query: evidence linking convulsions and retinal hemorrhages in neonates to serious underlying conditions

retrieved_docs.title: Retinal Hemorrhage -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Retinal hemorrhages in critically ill children with no history of trauma or abuse have a prevalence of 15%. Most cases are mild and are seen in children younger than two years and are associated with systemic infection, accidental brain trauma, and severe coagulopathy. [1] Birth-related retinal hemorrhages are seen in 25% of newborns with normal delivery and 40% to 50% of newborns with instrumental deliveries. [2] Retinal hemorrhages were seen in 30% of physically abused children, and most are less than six months of age. [3] [4] The prevalence of retinal hemorrhages in adults is most commonly seen after 40 years of age, as most systemic disorders are common in that age.

retrieved_docs.title: Subconjunctival Hemorrhage -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Physicians also play a key educational role in normalizing SCH for patients. Many individuals experience anxiety due to the abrupt, intense redness of the eye, fearing serious conditions such as stroke or retinal hemorrhage. Clear explanation of SCH anatomy and natural history reassures patients and equips them to recognize and respond to future occurrences appropriately. In pediatric cases, education focuses on caregivers, emphasizing the identification of patterns suggestive of trauma or bleeding disorders. Clinician confidence and clear communication are essential in these often benign but alarming conditions. Training students and residents to differentiate SCH from other causes of red eye through careful history-taking and physical examination is a critical component of medical education.

retrieved_docs.title: Herpes Simplex Neonatorum(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The cutaneous findings are scarring, active lesions, hypo, and hyperpigmentation of the skin, cutis aplasia, and macular rashes. In the eyes, one may see microphthalmia, retinal dysplasia, optic atrophy, and or chorioretinitis. Neurologically one can see microcephaly, encephalomalacia, hydranencephaly, and intracranial calcifications. Infants with disseminated disease often present in the first three weeks of life with symptoms of sepsis. Multiple organ systems can be affected by this illness, causing jaundice, abnormal liver function, hypoglycemia, hypotension, coagulopathy, pneumonia, and even respiratory failure. Encephalitis is present 75% of the time in disseminated disease, thus causing seizures.  Rarely do these infants present with vesicular lesions. Death usually results from shock, progressive liver failure, severe coagulopathy, respiratory failure, and progressive neurological deterioration. These infants usually present around the second or third week of life. They may initially have a fever, poor feeding, or present with a sudden episode of seizures or apnea. Between 60% and 70% of babies classified as having CNS disease have associated skin lesions at some point in the course of their illness. Skin, eye, and mouth disease also presents in the second to the third week of life. Typical vesicular lesions on an erythematous base are the presenting sign. Rarely are lesions present in the mouth. Conjunctivitis is often present and, left untreated, may develop into herpetic keratitis and possibly even corneal blindness. SEM has low mortality but reoccurs in 90% of patients. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Roth Spots -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: White centered retinal hemorrhages are a nonspecific ophthalmologic finding seen in multiple systemic conditions of various etiologies.  Roth spots are seen on fundoscopic examination and tend to be located in the peripheral or posterior poles of the eyes. Retinal endothelial dysfunction appears to be a common thread among the diverse causes of white centered retinal hemorrhage. The differential diagnosis of Roth spots is vast and includes: Bacterial endocarditis Anemia/thrombocytopenia Collagen vascular disease Leukemia Hypertensive retinopathy Diabetic retinopathy Pre-eclampsia Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) Anoxia Shaken baby syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: New York State Child Abuse, Maltreatment, and Neglect -- History and Physical -- Pediatric abusive head trauma
retrieved_docs.content: The long-term sequela of PAHT includes: Blindness Cerebral palsy Developmental delay Hearing loss Learning difficulties Seizures Glutaric aciduria type 1 is a rare metabolic disease that can cause retinal hemorrhages and subdural hematomas and may be mistaken for PAHT.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Abusive Head Trauma -- Introduction -- Shaken Baby Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: Key features of SBS include cerebral edema, retinal hemorrhage, and subdural hematoma (SDH). The presence of cerebral edema indicates the severity of injury. Retinal hemorrhages caused by accidental trauma are typically mild and require high-impact mechanisms, such as motor vehicle collisions. Results from a study conducted in France found that grade 3A and 3B hemorrhages were specific for SBS, with a high positive predictive value of 0.96. Hemorrhages extending beyond the posterior pole demonstrate high specificity for severe head injury, particularly AHT.

retrieved_docs.title: Purtscher Retinopathy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Amniotic fluid embolism may occur during childbirth or after birth. Fat embolism may occur after the fracture or crush injury of large bones. Acute pancreatitis may also cause fat embolism from enzymatic degradation of omental fat. Fat embolism syndrome (cerebral dysfunction, changes in the lung, and petechial hemorrhage) may be present in patients with fractures of long bones, and patients with fat embolism may have retinal manifestations. Features of fat embolism include respiratory distress, confusion/agitation, petechial rashes, and tachycardia. Air embolism from chest compression injury after various causes, including road traffic accident

retrieved_docs.title: Cortical Blindness -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cortical blindness can affect both children and adults. In children, common causes include: [5] [6] [7] [8] Traumatic brain injury to the occipital lobe of the brain Congenital abnormalities of the occipital lobe Perinatal ischemia In adults, it is seen in lesions of the primary visual cortex of the occipital lobes secondary to multiple disorders, including: Stroke Cardiac embolism Head trauma Occipital lobe epilepsy Hyponatremia Severe hypoglycemia Creutzfeldt-Jacob disease Infection e.g., HIV Eclampsia MELAS (mitochondrial myopathy, encephalopathy, lactic acidosis, and stroke-like episodes) Rarely, transient cortical blindness can be caused by Infective endocarditis or Hypertensive encephalopathy, Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES)

retrieved_docs.title: Menkes Disease -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Late complications of the disease include intractable seizures, subdural hematoma, blindness, and recurrent infections. In classical Menkes disease, death occurs by three years of age, usually secondary to vascular complications or respiratory infections. Renal complications may also occur secondary to copper sequestration in the proximal renal tubule. Growth failure becomes apparent shortly after the beginning of neurodegeneration. Ocular pathologies have been observed, such as retinal hypopigmentation, partial optic nerve atrophy, macular dystrophy, congenital cataracts, and microcysts in the iris.

retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Manifestations of Preeclampsia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, cortical visual loss can be caused by ischemic cortical disease. Patients with uncomplicated pregnancies experience a hypercoagulable state related to dehydration, hemoconcentration, and markedly upregulated clotting factor levels and experience stroke at three times the rate of age-matched non-pregnant women. Although the incidence of stroke is much lower in reproductive-age women, 7.7% of pregnancy-related deaths in the United States are caused by strokes, and worldwide this number ranges from 2.7% to 20% of pregnancies. Preeclampsia and eclampsia subsequently increase the risk of both hemorrhagic and ischemic stroke, increasing the risk between 2- and 5-fold. [29] [30] [31] [32] [33] Strokes associated with preeclampsia and eclampsia have been shown to occur most frequently after delivery. [34] Numerous case studies have shown visual disability in these patients stemming from parietal, occipital, and temporal lobe strokes. [35] [36] [37]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1089
retrieved_docs.content: with or lead to infectious complications around birth. Overall, the knowledge network portrays neonatal disorders as an interconnected group of conditions that include traumatic cerebral injury, intracranial and neonatal hemorrhage, perinatal infections, metabolic diseases such as transient neonatal diabetes, neurologic problems like developmental delay and epilepsy, and extreme prematurity. Birth trauma is a key antecedent of intracranial bleeding, which is classified under neonatal hemorrhage, while perinatal infection serves both as a parent condition for unspecified perinatal disorders and as a member of the broader neonatal disorder set. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment, diagnosis, and management of newborns presenting with any of these interrelated pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2282
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all identified as specific manifestations or related entities of مرض السكري (diabetes). In clinical practice, مرض السكري is linked not only to its primary metabolic disturbances but also to other pancreatic disorders that are distinct from diabetes itself, highlighting the broader spectrum of pancreatic pathology that may coexist with or mimic diabetic presentations. Additionally, مرض السكري is associated with اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder), reflecting the well‑known risk of diabetic retinopathy and other vision‑related complications in patients with diabetes. Furthermore, مرض السكري is recognized as having a child condition, متلازمة الأيض (metabolic syndrome), underscoring the interrelationship between diabetes and the cluster of metabolic risk factors that include obesity, hypertension, dyslipidemia, and insulin resistance. Together, these connections emphasize the heterogeneous nature of diabetes, its potential neonatal and developmental presentations, its association with other pancreatic and ocular disorders, and its role as a central component of metabolic syndrome, all of which are important considerations for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1640
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all mapped to مرض السكري, indicating that these specific neonatal diabetes conditions and the syndrome combining developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes are recognized as forms or manifestations of diabetes. In addition, the condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium” is an inverse‑isa relationship to مرض السكري, meaning that gestational or peripartum diabetes represents a particular clinical presentation of the disease. Clinically, مرض السكري is associated with pancreatic disorders (not diabetes), reflecting that patients with diabetes may also exhibit other pancreatic abnormalities unrelated to the primary diabetic process. Moreover, مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية (a retinal disorder) and اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ (diabetic retinopathy), underscoring the well‑known ocular complications of diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate the diverse forms of diabetes—including transient neonatal types and pregnancy‑related glucose intolerance—and their broader systemic associations with pancreatic and retinal pathology, which are important considerations for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all mapped to مرض السكري, indicating that these distinct neonatal and genetic forms of diabetes are considered subtypes of diabetes mellitus. مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, reflecting a general retinal disorder that can occur in diabetic patients, and more specifically with اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ (diabetic retinopathy), a hallmark microvascular complication of diabetes. In addition, مرض السكري is clinically associated with مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية (end‑stage renal disease), demonstrating that chronic diabetes can progress to severe kidney failure. The relationship between اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ and مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية further underscores the shared microvascular pathology that links diabetic eye disease and diabetic kidney disease, highlighting the importance of early detection and integrated management of these complications in patients with any form of diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2790

These specific neonatal and developmental presentations are therefore recognized as manifestations or subtypes of diabetes mellitus. In addition, مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, indicating that retinal disorders such as diabetic retinopathy commonly occur in patients with diabetes mellitus. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians identify atypical early‑onset forms of diabetes and monitor for ocular complications.

retrieved_docs.content: developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes within the same diagnostic framework as other neonatal disorders. Overall, the knowledge network connects perinatal insults—such as birth trauma, intracerebral hemorrhage, and foetal undernutrition—to a continuum of disorders ranging from metabolic (transient neonatal diabetes) to neurological (developmental delay, epilepsy, cerebral injury) conditions in infants and newborns. Recognizing these interrelationships is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and comprehensive patient care in the neonatal period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 535
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري) is presented as an overarching disorder that includes several specific entities: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder); Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1; and Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, all of which are mapped to مرض السكري. In addition, the condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium” is identified as a particular manifestation of diabetes, indicating that gestational or pregnancy‑related glucose intolerance falls under the same disease umbrella. Clinically, مرض السكري is associated with pancreatic disorders that are not themselves diabetes, highlighting a broader involvement of pancreatic pathology. It is also linked to retinal abnormalities, specifically اضطراب في الشبكية (a retinal disorder) and اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ (diabetic retinopathy), underscoring the eye complications commonly seen in diabetic patients. Metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض) is described as a child of مرض السكري, suggesting that metabolic syndrome can arise as a downstream or component condition of diabetes. Both مرض السكري and متلازمة الأيض share the GDC value “komorbidita,” reflecting their status as comorbid conditions. An additional entry labeled “Unknown” also carries the komorbidita value, indicating the presence of an unspecified comorbidity related to the same category. Overall, the knowledge base connects various neonatal and pregnancy‑related forms of diabetes, associated pancreatic and retinal disorders, and the comorbid relationship with metabolic syndrome, emphasizing the multifaceted nature of diabetes mellitus and its relevance for comprehensive clinical assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1950
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all recognized as specific forms or presentations that map to مرض السكري (diabetes). The condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium” is also identified as a manifestation of مرض السكري, emphasizing the impact of diabetes on maternal and perinatal health. Norgestrel is contraindicated in patients with مرض السكري, indicating that this contraceptive medication should be avoided in individuals with the disease. Additionally, اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder) is clinically associated with مرض السكري, reflecting the well‑known ocular complications of diabetes. Collectively, these relationships illustrate the diverse neonatal and developmental expressions of diabetes, its complications during pregnancy, medication safety considerations, and its association with retinal pathology.

Specific neonatal metabolic and neurologic disorders such as “DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder)” and “Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes” are each mapped to the umbrella term “اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة,” illustrating that these conditions are considered subtypes of newborn disorders. The “اضطرابات لدى حديث الولادة (SMQ)” (Standardized MedDRA Query for neonatal disorders) aggregates a collection of related entities, including the broader group “اضطراب لدى الرضع وحديثي الولادة” (infant and newborn disorder). Members of this SMQ include: - “ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}” (birth at maximal prematurity, weight unspecified), - “نزيف مخي وليدي” (intracranial hemorrhage in the newborn), and - “عدوى في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة، غير محدد بخلاف ذلك” (infection in the perinatal period, unspecified). Additional conditions linked to the infant and newborn

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 579
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) is presented as a central disease entity that encompasses a wide range of related conditions, complications, and associated diagnostic components. Several specific forms of diabetes, including DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and the syndrome described as Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, are all mapped to the broader category of diabetes, indicating that these neonatal and transient presentations are considered subtypes of the same metabolic disorder. Clinically, diabetes is linked to multiple serious complications. Retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) is clinically associated with diabetes, reflecting the well‑known risk of diabetic retinopathy. Congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني) is also clinically associated with diabetes, and the procedure of amputation of lower extremity is further linked to this cardiac condition, underscoring the systemic impact of the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1740
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosis/Diseases Component is classified under مرض السكري (diabetes mellitus). The specific disorders DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder) and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are both mapped to مرض السكري, indicating they are forms of transient neonatal diabetes that fall within the broader category of diabetes mellitus. The combined condition Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes is also mapped to مرض السكري, highlighting its association with neonatal diabetes as part of the diabetes spectrum. Clinically, اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder) is associated with مرض السكري, reflecting the well‑known ocular complications of diabetes. Additionally, متلازمة الأيض (metabolic syndrome) is identified as a child of مرض السكري, signifying that metabolic syndrome can arise as a downstream metabolic complication of diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate how various neonatal diabetes subtypes, developmental and neurological manifestations, ocular complications, and metabolic syndrome are interconnected within the overarching framework of diabetes mellitus, underscoring the importance of comprehensive assessment and management in patients with diabetes.

Consequently, extreme prematurity, birth‑related trauma leading to intracranial hemorrhage, and perinatal infections constitute interrelated components of neonatal morbidity, informing clinicians about the spectrum of conditions to consider when evaluating and managing newborn patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2634
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral injury due to birth trauma and DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder) are both classified as specific forms of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, the broader category of neonatal disorders. The condition Cerebral injury due to birth trauma represents a narrower, trauma‑related neurological injury that occurs in the newborn period, while transient neonatal diabetes mellitus is a metabolic disorder that appears shortly after birth and typically resolves. Recognizing that both entities fall under the umbrella of neonatal disorders highlights the need for targeted assessment and management strategies tailored to the unique pathophysiology of each condition in newborn patients.

retrieved_docs.content: Transient neonatal diabetes mellitus (DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL) and the syndrome “Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes” are mapped both to the neonatal disorder category and to diabetes mellitus, reflecting that a neonatal form of diabetes can coexist with developmental and epileptic features. Diabetes mellitus is further connected to non‑diabetic pancreatic disorders and retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية), underscoring its systemic impact. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 370
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري) is linked to a broad spectrum of related disorders and complications. Transient neonatal forms of diabetes, including DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 1 and DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2, as well as the syndrome of Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, are all mapped to مرض السكري, indicating they represent variants of the same metabolic disease. Clinically, مرض السكري is associated with retinal disorders such as اضطراب في الشبكية and اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ (diabetic retinopathy), and these retinal complications are further linked to end‑stage renal disease (مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية). In turn, مرض السكري itself is clinically associated with end‑stage renal disease, highlighting the kidney as a major target organ. Cardiovascular complications are also prominent: congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني) is clinically associated with مرض السكري, and amputation of lower extremity (amputation of lower extremity) is

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2731
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all specific forms of diabetes that map directly to مرض السكري (diabetes mellitus). The condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium” is also a manifestation of مرض السكري, representing gestational or pregnancy‑related glucose intolerance within the broader diabetes spectrum. Norgestrel is contraindicated in patients with مرض السكري, indicating that this emergency contraceptive should be avoided in individuals with diabetes. Retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) is clinically associated with مرض السكري, reflecting the well‑known risk of diabetic retinopathy in diabetic patients. Finally, malfunction of the human INSR protein (INSR protein, human) is linked to the development of مرض السكري, underscoring the role of insulin‑receptor defects in the pathogenesis of diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate the diverse clinical presentations, contraindications, complications, and molecular mechanisms associated with diabetes mellitus.
